deliverable_kind.description: ADR-0009: the kind of artifact this contract delivers. Selects which conformance profile is required and black-box checked against the built artifact. Declare it explicitly on every contract. Two non-checked values are deliberately distinct: 'none' asserts there is NO checkable interface (pure prose, data, a no-surface refactor) — correctly unverified; 'undetermined' asserts the deliverable HAS a checkable interface but of a kind no checker supports yet — it is recognized and streamed as unchecked (a checker is owed), NOT silently passed, and must not be downgraded to 'none' to dodge verification. Now SCHEMA-REQUIRED: every contract must declare its kind (the shadow-first pre-flight obligation, promoted to a hard requirement once the profiles cover all kinds — 'undetermined' covers any new kind, 'none' covers no-interface, so the field is always expressible).

forbidden_patterns.description: ADR-0009 / ADR-0016 D7: a CHEAP, deterministic (~0-FP) conformance gate, applicable to ANY deliverable_kind. Each entry declares a token (literal or regex) that must appear at most max_occurrences times in the PRODUCED artifact tree, ignoring files matching `exclude`. The gate greps the produced tree and emits a BLOCKING finding (source 'forbidden-pattern') when the count exceeds the budget — catching the straggler an incomplete rename/refactor leaves behind, which Linon would otherwise have to read the regex to find. grep is a FACT, so this moves a deterministically-catchable defect off the expensive semantic reviewer. Absent field = no-op (the check never fabricates a finding it cannot ground).

pattern.description: The token that must be (near-)absent from the produced tree. Compiled as a regex; if it is not valid regex it is treated as a literal substring. A bare identifier like `_scaffold_seed_commit` works either way.

exclude.description: Glob patterns (fnmatch over the workspace-relative path) whose matching files are ignored — e.g. the file that legitimately still defines the old name, a changelog, or generated fixtures. Keep narrow: an over-broad exclude defeats the gate.

max_occurrences.description: The maximum number of matches allowed across the (non-excluded) tree before the gate blocks (default 0 — the token must be fully gone).

reason.description: Why this pattern is forbidden — surfaced in the finding so a reader (and the repair routing) understands the obligation.

regression_suite.description: ADR-0009 / ADR-0016: a CHEAP, deterministic (~0-FP) conformance gate for the BIGGEST modification-defect class — 'the change broke previously-working code' (SWE-CI: ~73.6% of modification-task failures). Names a PRE-EXISTING repo test command that must still pass (exit 0) AFTER the change. Applicable to ANY deliverable_kind. The gate runs the command via the conformance runner against the produced artifact and emits a BLOCKING finding (source 'regression') capturing the command, exit code, and an output tail (failing test names if present) when it exits non-zero. A passing suite is a FACT — it either passes or it doesn't — so this moves a deterministically-catchable regression off the expensive semantic reviewer (Linon). Absent field = no-op (the gate never fabricates a finding it cannot ground).

command.description: The pre-existing repo test command that must still pass (exit 0) after the change — e.g. `python3 -m pytest -q` or `python3 scripts/test_foo.py`. Run as a shell string through the conformance runner (the box is the containment boundary, ADR-0022).

timeout_seconds.description: Wall-clock budget for the suite, passed to the runner (default ~300). Keeps a hung or runaway suite from stalling the gate.

reason.description: Why this suite must keep passing — surfaced in the finding so a reader (and the repair routing) understands the obligation.

static_checks.description: ADR-0009 / ADR-0016: a CHEAP, deterministic (~0-FP) conformance gate for the HALLUCINATION / INCONSISTENCY defect class — invented or undefined APIs (~26%), undefined variables (~16.9%), and outright syntax errors. A LIST of declared static analyzers, each of which MUST exit 0 on the produced artifact (e.g. `python3 -m py_compile cockpit/server.py`, `python3 -m pyflakes .`, `ruff check`, `tsc --noEmit`). Applicable to ANY deliverable_kind. The gate runs each command via the conformance runner against the produced artifact and emits a BLOCKING finding (source 'static-check') per failing analyzer, capturing the command, exit code, and an output tail (the analyzer's reported errors) when it exits non-zero. An analyzer's exit code is a FACT — it either resolves/parses or it doesn't — so this moves a deterministically-catchable hallucination off the expensive semantic reviewer (Linon). Empty / absent list = no-op (the gate never fabricates a finding it cannot ground).

command.description: The static-analysis command that must exit 0 on the produced artifact — e.g. `python3 -m py_compile cockpit/server.py`, `python3 -m pyflakes .`, `ruff check`, or `tsc --noEmit`. Run as a shell string through the conformance runner (the box is the containment boundary, ADR-0022).

timeout_seconds.description: Wall-clock budget for this analyzer, passed to the runner (default ~120). Keeps a hung or runaway analyzer from stalling the gate.

reason.description: Why this analyzer must pass — surfaced in the finding so a reader (and the repair routing) understands the obligation.

conformance.description: ADR-0009: the EXECUTABLE part of the contract — what the deterministic gate runs against the built artifact. A discriminated profile per deliverable_kind; only the kind's own profile is required (oneOf-style, so the schema never degrades into a universal checklist).

allOf.$comment: When this contract declares a CLI deliverable, it MUST carry a checkable cli conformance profile — the design agent chooses and encodes the interface; the gate verifies it (ADR-0009).
A http_service deliverable MUST carry a checkable http_service conformance profile (ADR-0009).
A library deliverable MUST carry a checkable library conformance profile (ADR-0009).
A json deliverable MUST carry a checkable json conformance profile (ADR-0009).
A batch_job deliverable MUST carry a checkable batch_job conformance profile (ADR-0009).
An rpc_service deliverable MUST carry a checkable rpc_service conformance profile (ADR-0009).

cli_profile.description: Black-box CLI contract: declared install, entrypoint, exit-code policy, and concrete invocation->outcome examples. The checker installs via the commands, runs each example, and compares exit status + stdout/stderr. Exit code is pinned because it is the largest observed interface leak.

commands.description: Shell commands that build/install the artifact in a clean environment, run in order before the examples. A non-zero exit is a critical conformance finding (the artifact cannot be built).

invocation.description: How the CLI is launched (e.g. `mytool` or `python -m mypkg`). Examples' invocations are interpreted relative to this entrypoint.

status_and_errors.description: The exit-code policy the examples are expected to honour. Recorded so the checker (and a reviewer) can see the intended categories, not just per-example codes.

examples.description: Concrete golden invocations. Each pins the exit status; stdout/stderr are checked by substring (preferred — robust to volatile output) or, when given, by normalized exact match. Avoid indiscriminate whole-output snapshots.

invocation.description: Arguments appended to the entrypoint (e.g. `--help`, `build bad-input`). The empty string is the no-argument invocation.

expected_stdout.description: Exact stdout after normalization (trailing whitespace, CRLF). Use sparingly; prefer expected_stdout_contains.

library_profile.description: Black-box library contract: import the built module and assert its declared public surface resolves. The checker runs an import probe (import the module, then check each exported symbol is present) THROUGH the same runner as the CLI checker — it never imports the built artifact in-process. Signatures and an API-diff against a baseline are a later addition; this first cut pins module-importability + exported-symbol presence, the load-bearing 'the public surface exists' contract.

commands.description: Shell commands that build/install the package in a clean environment, run in order before the import probe. A non-zero exit is a critical conformance finding.

module.description: The importable module path the probe imports, e.g. `jsonpick` or `mypkg.core`.

import_paths.description: Directories prepended to sys.path before the import, relative to the workspace (default: the workspace root). Use when the package is importable from a source dir without being installed.

exported_symbols.description: Attribute names that MUST resolve on the imported module (functions, classes, constants) — the declared public surface. Each missing one is a major finding.

python.description: Interpreter used to run the import probe when language is `python` (default `python3`).

language.description: The module's language, selecting how the public surface is introspected: `python` (default) imports the module and checks attributes via the python interpreter; `powershell` imports it with `Import-Module` and checks the exported commands via `pwsh`. Either way `module` names the module (or path) and `exported_symbols` are the names that must resolve.

http_service_profile.description: Black-box HTTP service contract: declared start command, readiness URL, and concrete request->response examples. The checker starts the real service process, waits until base_url returns any HTTP response, then compares status and capped body substrings for each example. At least one example must be a production-boundary end-to-end probe: launch command + externally observable endpoint + expected status/body.

command.description: Shell command used to start the service in the conformance sandbox.

base_url.description: Base URL polled for readiness and used to resolve example paths.

readiness_timeout_seconds.description: Maximum time to wait for base_url to produce any HTTP response before skipping derived example checks.

examples.description: Concrete golden HTTP requests. Each pins the response status; at least one also pins body content so a stubbed production path cannot satisfy the service boundary by returning only a generic status.

json.description: Optional JSON request body sent with the example.

rpc_service_profile.description: Black-box RPC contract: boot the service and ACTUALLY INVOKE each declared call over the declared transport (not a static shape check). `json_rpc_http` posts JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdlib HTTP and checks the result/error (an application error rides HTTP 200, so the HTTP status alone is not the contract); `grpc` invokes dynamically via a compiled descriptor set, with grpcio imported lazily only when a grpc deliverable is checked. At least one call must be a production-boundary end-to-end probe: launch command + externally observable call + expected result/error. Buf-style backwards-compatibility diffing against a baseline is a later addition.

command.description: Shell command that starts the service (run through the runner; stopped after the calls).

base_url.description: The endpoint: a full URL for json_rpc_http (the JSON-RPC POST target, also polled for readiness), or host:port for grpc.

readiness_timeout_seconds.description: How long to wait for json_rpc_http readiness before invoking (default 0).

descriptor_set_path.description: grpc only: workspace-relative path to a compiled FileDescriptorSet (e.g. `protoc --descriptor_set_out` / `buf build -o`) used to invoke methods dynamically.

calls.description: Concrete RPC calls to invoke. At least one pins either expected result content or an expected error code, so a declared service/integration boundary is actually exercised by conformance.

method.description: The JSON-RPC method name, or the gRPC `package.Service/Method` path.

params.description: The request params (JSON-RPC) or request message (gRPC, mapped into the protobuf message).

expected_result_contains.description: Substrings that must appear in the JSON-serialized result.

expected_error_code.description: When set, the call MUST fail with this error code (JSON-RPC error.code / gRPC status).

batch_job_profile.description: Black-box batch-job contract: run the job once and assert its exit status plus the artifacts it was supposed to produce. The closest sibling to the CLI checker — it runs through the same runner (the inner-box exec in production) — but its semantics are run-then-inspect-outputs rather than per-example invocation, so it is a distinct profile. Artifact existence is checked THROUGH the runner (a `test -e` in the run environment), so it holds inside the box, not only on the host.

commands.description: Shell commands that build/install the job in a clean environment, run in order before the job. A non-zero exit is a critical conformance finding.

command.description: The full shell command that runs the job once.

expected_status.description: The exit code the job must return (default 0). A mismatch is a major finding.

produced_artifacts.description: Paths (relative to the run environment) that MUST exist after the job runs — the outputs it was supposed to produce. Each missing one is a major finding. Existence is checked through the runner, so it holds inside the execution sandbox.

json_profile.description: Black-box contract for a produced JSON document. The checker reads each declared file, parses it, validates it against a declared JSON Schema (inline or referenced), and asserts declared key paths resolve. It is the only profile that runs NO process — pure structural validation of the produced data — so it executes nothing untrusted and needs no isolation. Use it when the deliverable IS a JSON artifact (config, API spec, schema, manifest, data fixture); a CLI/service that merely emits JSON stays a cli/http_service deliverable and is checked through its own profile's output assertions.

files.description: The produced JSON files to validate. Each names a path and, optionally, the shape it must satisfy.

path.description: Workspace-relative path to the produced JSON file. A missing file is a critical finding (nothing was delivered); an unparseable one is critical (not valid JSON).

schema.description: An inline JSON Schema the file's content must validate against. Each validation error is a major finding. Mutually exclusive with schema_path.

schema_path.description: Workspace-relative path to a JSON Schema file the content must validate against. Mutually exclusive with schema.

required_paths.description: Dotted key paths that MUST resolve in the parsed document (e.g. `info.version`, `paths`). Each missing one is a major finding. Cheaper than a full schema when only presence matters.
